gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to engineer the non-conventional, acid-tolerant yeast Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid (LA) production under low-pH conditions, addressing limitations of lactic acid bacteria (LAB) and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. LAB requires expensive neutralization agents and complex nutrients; S. cerevisiae is Crabtree-positive and sensitive to low pH. K. marxianus is GRAS, fast-growing, acid-tolerant (pH 3–8), and can utilize lignocellulosic sugars. The hypothesis is that exploiting natural diversity, genetic engineering, and adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) can yield strains with high LA titers, yields, and tolerance, reducing neutralization needs and enabling use of cheap feedstocks.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Overview: PLA is a promising bioplastic from LA. Current LA production via LAB requires neutralization agents. This study explores K. marxianus for LA production. Screened 168 strains, selected 10 for engineering (pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration, cyb2Δ). Best strain Km3 further improved by ALE, reaching 120 g/L LA, yield 0.81 g/g, with less neutralization and xylose utilization. Genome sequencing identified SUA7 mutation causal for improved performance.
process_signals: Initial problem: LAB fermentation requires neutralization agents and complex nutrients.
Hypothesis: K. marxianus acid tolerance can reduce neutralization needs.
Approach: Screen diversity → engineer → evolve.
Key result: ALE increased LA production by 18%.
Causal mutation: SUA7.
methods: Genetic engineering: pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration, cyb2Δ
Adaptive laboratory evolution under LA stress
Whole genome sequencing for SNP identification
metrics: 18% increase in LA production after ALE

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: PLA production relies on LA, mostly from LAB fermentation, which requires neutralization agents (e.g., CaCO3) and complex nutrients. S. cerevisiae is a common cell factory but is Crabtree-positive (ethanol production under high sugar) and sensitive to low pH, making it suboptimal for LA. K. marxianus is an alternative: GRAS, fast growth (doubling time 0.75 h⁻¹), acid-tolerant (pH 3–8), metabolizes xylose/arabinose, and has a growing genetic toolbox. The study aims to exploit K. marxianus diversity and engineering for LA production.
process_signals: Problem: LAB neutralization and nutrient requirements limit sustainability.
S. cerevisiae limitations: Crabtree effect, low pH sensitivity.
K. marxianus advantages: acid tolerance, xylose utilization, GRAS.
Hypothesis: K. marxianus can be engineered for low-pH LA production.
failures_or_limits: S. cerevisiae not ideal for low-pH LA fermentations.

section_gists.heading: High-throughput screening of 168 K. marxianus strains reveals large phenotypic diversity
section_gists.gist: Screened 168 strains on 19 conditions (stress, carbon sources, inhibitors). Large phenotypic diversity observed. Growth at pH 2.5 was possible but reduced; at 50 g/L LA, only two strains (Km3, Km4) grew. Selected 10 diverse, robust strains including benchmark NBRC 1777 for engineering.
process_signals: Screening rationale: identify robust chassis for LA production.
Key finding: only Km3 and Km4 grew at 50 g/L LA.
Selection criteria: overall robustness, diversity, LA tolerance.
Benchmark: NBRC 1777 included.
metrics: Growth at pH 2.5: 18% smaller colonies vs pH 3.5
failures_or_limits: Most strains failed to grow at 50 g/L LA, indicating LA toxicity is a major hurdle.

section_gists.heading: Genetic engineering enables LA production
section_gists.gist: Engineered 10 selected strains: pdc1Δ, LpLDH integration at PDC1 locus, cyb2Δ. pdc1Δ+LDH enabled LA production in all strains; cyb2Δ increased titers up to 2-fold. Best strain Km3^eng produced 14.8 g/L LA, yield 0.68 g/g in 16h flask with 2% glucose. Strain background strongly affected production (1.7–14.8 g/L). Acetic acid production varied; no glycerol detected.
process_signals: Engineering strategy: delete PDC1 to increase pyruvate, integrate LDH, delete CYB2 to prevent LA consumption.
cyb2Δ doubled LA titers.
Strain background crucial: Km3 best.
Byproduct: acetic acid still produced despite pdc1Δ.
methods: Shake flask assays in rich medium with 2% glucose
failures_or_limits: LA titers still low (max 14.8 g/L) and likely limited by LA toxicity.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution improved LA tolerance and production
section_gists.gist: ALE of Km3^eng under LA stress (no pH control) in three populations (A,B,C) plus control. LA concentration increased stepwise (15→30 g/L). After 52–104 generations, single colonies screened; all evolved strains grew at 30 g/L LA vs. parental only at 10 g/L. 13/24 selected evolved strains produced more LA. Whole genome sequencing of best clones: SNPs in SUA7, BCK1, etc. Reverse engineering: reverting SUA7 mutation reduced LA tolerance and production, confirming causality. SUA7^S279P mutation improved LA tolerance, production, and acetic acid tolerance. BCK1 mutation also beneficial. Combining SUA7* and BCK1* further improved growth in LA and acetic acid.
process_signals: Hypothesis: LA tolerance limits production; ALE can improve it.
ALE setup: increasing LA stress, no pH control, parallel populations.
Evolved strains gained ability to grow at 30 g/L LA.
SUA7 mutation causal: reversion reduced tolerance/production.
SUA7^S279P also improved acetic acid tolerance.
Combining SUA7* and BCK1* additive effect.
methods: ALE in medium with L-LA, stepwise increase (15→20→25→30 g/L)
Single colony isolation and screening in LA concentrations
Whole genome sequencing and SNP identification
Reverse engineering by reverting SNPs to wild type
metrics: Evolved strains grew at 30 g/L LA vs. parental 10 g/L
failures_or_limits: Not all evolved strains improved LA production (13/24).
Multiple SNPs per strain; only SUA7 and BCK1 validated.

section_gists.heading: Engineered strains reach industrially relevant LA titers in fed-batch fermentations
section_gists.gist: Fed-batch bioreactor fermentations (0.5 L, pH 6.0, DO>20%) with Km3^eng and SNP-introduced strains. Km3^eng reached 116 g/L LA, yield 0.83 g/g, productivity 1.39 g/L/h. Km3^eng,SUA7*: 120 g/L, yield 0.81 g/g, productivity 1.44 g/L/h, max productivity 3.43 g/L/h. Differences not statistically significant. Byproducts varied: SUA7* produced more acetic acid but less succinic acid. Low-pH fermentations: at pH 4.5, Km3^eng,SUA7* produced 94 g/L (yield 0.79 g/g); at pH 3.5, Km3^eng produced 54 g/L (yield 0.68 g/g).
process_signals: Validation in bioreactors at pH 6.0 (lower than industrial LAB pH 7.0).
SUA7* strain showed slightly higher LA titer and productivity.
Low-pH performance: still significant LA production at pH 4.5 and 3.5, but reduced.
Byproduct trade-offs: SUA7* increased acetic acid.
metrics: Km3^eng: 116 g/L, yield 0.83 g/g, prod 1.39 g/L/h
Km3^eng,SUA7*: 120 g/L, yield 0.81 g/g, prod 1.44 g/L/h
failures_or_limits: No statistically significant difference between strains at pH 6.0.
LA production decreased at lower pH.
Acetic acid byproduct increased in SUA7* strain.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Screening revealed diversity; agave isolates robust. Growth at pH 2.5 but not at 50 g/L LA suggests combined low pH and LA toxicity. Engineering: pdc1Δ abolished ethanol; cyb2Δ doubled LA. Acetic acid still produced via mitochondrial Ach1. ALE improved LA tolerance; SUA7^S279P mutation in transcription factor IIB enhanced LA tolerance and production, possibly via oxidative stress response. BCK1 mutation in cell wall integrity pathway also beneficial. Combining mutations additive. Low-pH fermentations show potential but need further improvement. Future: exploit xylose utilization for second-generation LA.
process_signals: Interpretation of screening: combined low pH and LA toxicity is key hurdle.
Acetic acid production explained by Ach1 activity.
SUA7 role: transcription factor IIB, may regulate oxidative stress genes.
BCK1 role: cell wall integrity, may improve stress tolerance.
Low-pH performance still suboptimal; further engineering needed.
Future direction: lignocellulosic feedstocks.
failures_or_limits: Only two mutations validated; other SNPs may contribute.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: K. marxianus is a promising LA producer. Combining biodiversity screening, genetic engineering, and ALE yielded strains with high LA production and stress resilience, enabling cheaper, more sustainable processes. Future work: leverage xylose metabolism for second-generation LA production.
process_signals: Success of integrated approach: screen → engineer → evolve.
Potential for lignocellulosic feedstocks.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study follows a logical progression: (1) Identify problem (LAB limitations) and propose K. marxianus as solution due to acid tolerance and xylose utilization. (2) Screen natural diversity to find robust chassis, revealing that LA toxicity is a major hurdle. (3) Engineer LA production pathway, but titers remain low due to LA toxicity. (4) Apply ALE to improve LA tolerance, identifying key mutations (SUA7, BCK1). (5) Validate in bioreactors, showing industrially relevant titers but with trade-offs (acetic acid). (6) Conclude that combined approach is effective and point to future improvements (low-pH, lignocellulose).
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the exact mechanism by which SUA7^S279P improves LA tolerance?
Are there other beneficial mutations in the evolved strains that were not validated?
Can further ALE or engineering eliminate acetic acid byproduct?
How to improve low-pH (≤4.5) LA production to avoid neutralization entirely?
What is the performance on real lignocellulosic hydrolysates?

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid production under low-pH conditions to reduce neutralization costs and enable use of cheap feedstocks.
